data.title: Vistra Corp. CEO James A. Burke Sells Over $7 Million in Company Stock
data.description:
Reporter NameBurke James A
RelationshipPresident and CEO
TypeSell
Amount$7,724,187
SEC FilingForm 4

James A. Burke, President and CEO of Vistra Corp., reported selling 39,201 shares of common stock across transactions on September 30 and October 1, 2025, totaling $7,724,187. The sales were executed at weighted-average prices of $199.3 and $195.0, with some sales also at $192.87. Following these transactions, Burke directly owns 253,815 shares and indirectly owns 735,773 shares through various trusts and partnerships. The sales were conducted under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on June 12, 2025.

SEC Filing: Vistra Corp. [ VST ] - Form 4 - Oct. 02, 2025

data.title: SEMrush Holdings Director Dmitry Melnikov Sells Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameMelnikov Dmitry
RelationshipDirector, 10% Owner
TypeSell
Amount$10,067
SEC FilingForm 4

Dmitry Melnikov, a 10% owner and director of SEMrush Holdings, sold 1,408 shares of Class A Common Stock on October 1, 2025, at a weighted average price of $7.15 per share, totaling $10,067. Following the transaction, Melnikov directly owns 1,281,956 shares and indirectly owns 17,599,855 shares through various trusts and entities. The sale was conducted to meet tax obligations related to the vesting of restricted stock units.

SEC Filing: SEMrush Holdings, Inc. [ SEMR ] - Form 4 - Oct. 02, 2025

data.title: United Therapeutics Executives Engage in Significant Stock Transactions
data.description:
Reporter NameRelationshipTypeAmountSEC Filing
Rothblatt Martine AChairperson & CEOSell$3,441,321Form 4
Mahon Paul AEVP & GENERAL COUNSELSell$4,802,573Form 4

Martine A. Rothblatt, Chairperson & CEO of United Therapeutics, has sold 8,000 shares of common stock on October 1 and 2, 2025, under a pre-arranged 10b5-1 trading plan. The transactions were executed at weighted average prices ranging from $423.3225 to $436.7, totaling $3,441,321. Following these transactions, Rothblatt directly owns 130 shares and indirectly owns 644,359 shares through various family trusts.

Paul A. Mahon, EVP & General Counsel of United Therapeutics Corp, sold 11,000 shares of Common Stock on October 2, 2025, under a pre-arranged 10b5-1 plan. The transactions were executed at weighted average prices ranging from $433.7234 to $440.1604, totaling approximately $4,802,573. Following these sales, Mahon directly owns 36,781 shares of United Therapeutics Corp.

data.title: Soybeans rebound as market weighs China prospects
data.description:
Key points:
  • Soybeans higher on hopes for Chinese demand
  • Wheat rises on good demand, corn follows wheat higher

By Heather Schlitz

Chicago soybean futures ticked higher on Thursday, continuing the previous day's rebound as the market assessed chances for a resumption in Chinese demand following comments by U.S. President Donald Trump, analysts said.

Corn and wheat, which like soybeans hit multiweek lows on Wednesday, extended a bounce, with help from a weaker dollar and signs of fresh demand from wheat importers.

The most-active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) settled 10-3/4 cents higher at $10.23-3/4 per bushel. On Wednesday, the contract fell below $10 for the first time in seven weeks before rebounding by the close.

U.S. farmers have lost out on billions of dollars of sales to China, which is the biggest soy importer but has not yet bought beans from the autumn U.S. harvest amid a trade war with Washington.

Trump said in a social media post on Wednesday that soybeans would be a major topic of discussion when he meets Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping in four weeks.

"That gave soybeans support yesterday and we're building on that today," Karl Setzer, partner at Consus Ag, said. Rising soymeal prices have also provided support for soybeans.

The post spurred some buying, but ample supply and China's continued absence from the U.S. market will likely cap any gains.

Ignoring the U.S., China has ramped up soybean purchases from South America, including a large volume of Argentine soybeans last week during a brief export tax waiver.

CBOT corn settled 5-1/4 cents higher at $4.21-1/2 per bushel, and CBOT wheat settled 5-1/2 cents higher at $5.14-3/4 per bushel.

Strong demand for wheat and signs of planting delays for winter wheat in the U.S. Plains have given a boost to wheat futures, with corn following wheat higher.

A U.S. government shutdown since Wednesday, amid political deadlock over budget funding, will likely delay the release of key USDA data.

data.title: XOMA Royalty Extends Tender Offer To Acquire LAVA Therapeutics N.V. >XOMA
Police use tear gas and water canons on Gaza protest in Geneva
data.description:
By Olivia Le Poidevin and Emma Farge

Police sprayed tear gas on protesters in Geneva, Switzerland on Thursday evening who were marching in support of the Gaza flotilla, eyewitnesses told Reuters, which the police confirmed.

"We were retreating and suddenly got heavily gassed," a eye witness told Reuters, describing a burning sensation in their eyes and difficult breathing.

Israel's interception of the flotilla sparked protests in Geneva and Bern, as well as in Italy and Colombia on Thursday.

Israeli forces intercepted around 40 boats carrying aid and more than 400 foreign activists, including some Swiss nationals, and Swedish campaigner Greta Thunberg, to Gaza

A photo shared with Reuters by a eyewitness in Geneva showed a large plume of white smoke coming from a street filled with protesters, which they described as tear gas.

A second eyewitness told Reuters that they saw a large white vehicle spray water on the front line of protesters, which a video shared by the Swiss national broadcaster RTS also showed.

Alexandre Brahier, spokesperson for the Geneva police said that around 3,000 people, mostly adults and young men had joined the protest.

"We had to use control measures including tear gas and water canons," he said, adding that protesters were not carrying dangerous arms but were "throwing things", causing some damage to property spraying graffiti.

Such confrontations are rare for Switzerland although pro-Gaza protests have been gathering momentum in recent weeks.

"Large protests are to be expected in big cities but it’s not every day that we need to resort to control measures like this," Brahier added.

Mobile phone footage shared with Reuters show flares being thrown amidst the crowd, as hundreds of people moving down a crammed street in central Geneva.

data.title: Coursera CFO to Leave Role
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Coursera Chief Financial Officer Ken Hahn will leave his role, effective Oct. 29, and become an adviser to the company.

The online learning company said it had begun a search to identify a permanent replacement.

Hahn has served as CFO of Coursera since 2020 and is credited with leading the company's successful initial public offering in 2021. He will stay on as an adviser for one year.

Last year, Hahn's total compensation was $9.2 million, including a base salary of $446,833, according to an annual filing from the company.

The company reaffirmed its previous guidance for the third quarter and full year.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: Heard on the Street Recap: Taking Credit — WSJ
data.description:
By Heard Editors

What Happened in Markets Today:

OpenAI reaches $500 billion valuation. A group of investors paid about $6.6 billion to buy shares owned by OpenAI employees, valuing the company at $500 billion. The company also reached deals with Korean companies Samsung and SK Hynix as memory-chip partners to the Stargate infrastructure project.

Chip stocks rose. The rally wasn't limited to Samsung and SK Hynix, which rose 3.5% and 9.9%, respectively. AMD and Intel shares gained 3.5% and 3.8%, respectively.

Tesla hit a sales record. The company's global EV deliveries rose 7.4% in the third quarter compared with a year earlier as U.S. consumers rushed to take advantage of the $7,500 federal EV credit. The credit expired at the end of the quarter. Tesla shares fell 5.1%.

FICO introduced a program that cuts out credit bureaus. The program would let mortgage lenders buy credit scores directly from the company, bypassing Experian, Equifax and TransUnion. FICO shares surged 18%, making it the top performer in the S&P 500. TransUnion and Equifax shares fell 11% and 8.5%, respectively. Experian was down 4.2%.

The government shutdown continued a second day. Lawmakers remain deadlocked over spending. Prediction markets suggest that bettors think the government shutdown will last until at least the end of next week.

This analysis comes from the Journal's Heard on the Street team. Subscribe to their free daily afternoon newsletter here.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Turkey may work jointly with US, S.Korea for its second nuclear power plant, minister says
data.description:
Turkey may work with the United States and South Korea for its second planned nuclear power plant, Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar said on Thursday, adding this may be in the form of a trilateral model.

Ankara has said it was in talks with Russia, China, Canada and South Korea on possible new nuclear power plants, in the northern Sinop and Western Thrace regions, to add to the Akkuyu nuclear power plant it is building with Russia.

Speaking to broadcaster CNN Turk, Bayraktar said President Tayyip Erdogan had discussed cooperation on both small and big reactors with the leaders of Canada and France, adding that the country would cooperate with the United States for both small modular and conventional plant development after a meeting with U.S. President Donald Trump at the White House last month.

"We can say the United States and (South) Korea got added together. Therefore, there may be a trilateral model with Korea-America-Turkey," he said, while repeating that the first reactor of Akkuyu would be operational in 2026.

Bayraktar also said Turkey wanted access to cheap energy and technology transfer, know-how with such investments, and that an accord providing these was reached with Russia on Akkuyu, so it would also seek the same for its second nuclear power plant.

data.title: Cidara Gets $339 Million from HHS for Non-Vaccine Influenza Preventative
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Cidara received an award of up to $339 million from the Department of Health and Human Services to support its development of a non-vaccine preventative of influenza.

The company will receive $58 million over two years to support its onshoring of manufacturing of the antiviral drug, called CD388.

HHS could provide an additional $281 million if requested by Cidara to support studies of the drug in specific populations

The award comes from the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development authority within HHS.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: NTSB, FAA to probe crashes of two Amazon delivery drones
data.description:
By David Shepardson

Two U.S. agencies said Thursday they will investigate the collision of two Amazon Prime Air delivery drones with the boom of a crane in Tolleson, Arizona.

The National Transportation Safety Board and Federal Aviation Administration both said they will investigate the incident that occurred on Wednesday.

Amazon in November 2024 announced it had begun drone deliveries in the West Valley of the Phoenix Metro Area in Arizona from its same-day site in Tolleson and said Thursday it has temporarily paused Prime Air drone operations in Arizona.

Amazon spokesman Terrence Clark said the company was aware of the incident and "currently working with the relevant authorities to investigate."

Amazon says customers who live near the Tolleson site can purchase an eligible item weighing 5 pounds or less and have it delivered by drone in under an hour.

In 2023, Amazon began delivering by drone prescription medications in partnership with Amazon Pharmacy to customers in College Station, Texas.

In August, the U.S. Transportation Department proposed new rules to speed deployment of drones beyond the visual line of sight of operators, a key change needed to advance commercial uses like package deliveries.

"It's going to change the way that people and products move throughout our airspace... so you may change the way you get your Amazon package, you may get a Starbucks cup of coffee from a drone," Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said.

Amazon has a goal of delivering 500 million packages annually by drone by the end of 2030.

data.title: Grades slip as WTI/Brent spread narrows
data.description:
Grades broadly slipped on Thursday, dealers said, as the spread between U.S. crude futures and the global benchmark Brent narrowed, weighing on export demand.

U.S. crude futures' discount to Brent narrowed to minus $3.95 a barrel. A narrower spread makes U.S. grades less attractive to foreign buyers. Earlier in the session, the discount narrowed to as little as minus $3.87, its smallest since August 29, according to data from LSEG.

Minus $4 per barrel is typically considered the level that encourages U.S. exports and drives grades higher, as traders see an open arbitrage route.

OPEC's oil output rose further in September after an OPEC+ agreement to raise production, a Reuters survey found on Thursday, mainly due to higher production by the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia.

  • Light Louisiana Sweet for November delivery fell 7 cents to a midpoint of a $2.23 premium and was seen bid and offered between a $2.15 and $2.30 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures

  • Mars Sour was steady at a midpoint of a 55-cent discount and was seen bid and offered between a 65-cent and 45-cent a barrel discount to U.S. crude futures

  • WTI Midland fell 10 cents to a midpoint of a 75-cent premium and was seen bid and offered between a 65-cent and 85-cent a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures ​

  • West Texas Sour fell 20 cents to a midpoint of parity and was seen bid and offered between a discount of 15 cents​​ and a 15-cent a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures ​

  • WTI at East Houston, also known as MEH, traded between a 95-cent and $1.15 a barrel premium to U.S. crude futures ​

  • ICE Brent December futures fell $1.24 to settle at $64.11 a barrel

  • WTI November crude futures fell $1.30 to settle at $60.48 a barrel

  • The Brent/WTI spread narrowed 1 cent to last trade at minus $3.95, after hitting a high of minus $3.87 and a low of minus $4.01

data.title: CACI International Secures $73M U.S. Air Force Contract
data.description:
By Terell Wright

CACI International has won a $73 million, five-year contract to continue its work modernizing the U.S. Air Force's integrated broadcast service.

The national security technology company said Thursday that the contract from the Defense Department will focus on upgrading the Air Force's network for transmitting time-sensitive tactical and strategic intelligence and targeting data across multiple domains, including air, ground and space, for the Defense Department and U.S. allies.

The contract will support the Integrated Broadcast Services-Network Services, which are used to transmit intelligence for the Air Force electronically.

CACI Chief Executive John Mengucci said the company's software-defined solutions that broaden the Air Force's network capabilities will bolster the nation's national security and allow it to maintain superiority.

Write to Terell Wright at terell.wright@wsj.com

data.title: Rumble Enters Strategic Partnership with AI-Powered Perplexity
data.description:
By Emon Reiser

Rumble is tapping into Perplexity's artificial intelligence tools with a strategic partnership aimed at improving video discovery for users and creators on its platform.

The Longboat Key, Fla.-based company said on Thursday that Perplexity's technology will help its users discover relevant content, a boon for both creators seeking to reach their audiences and viewers looking for specific topics and discussions.

As part of the collaboration, Perplexity's new browser, Comet, will be promoted to Rumble's users. Rumble is also introducing a new subscription bundle combining Rumble Premium and Perplexity Pro.

"Every video platform faces the challenge of connecting viewers with content that matches their interests," said Dmitry Shevelenko, Perplexity's chief business officer. "Our search technology is designed to understand user intent and surface relevant results efficiently."

Write to Emon Reiser at emon.reiser@wsj.com

data.title: These Are the 4 Kinds of High-Performing Financial Advisors. What Type Are You? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Dimple Shah

The organic growth conundrum facing advisors today reflects the convergence of economic headwinds, changing client expectations, and a competitive field. Markets remain unpredictable, client needs are becoming more complex and new entrants are crowding the wealth management space. Advisors are being asked to deliver tax, estate, and succession planning alongside investment advice — all while demonstrating digital sophistication.

Despite these challenges, some advisors are thriving. In an effort to understand what distinguishes these high performing wealth managers from their peers, my firm conducted a study of over 5,700 affiliated financial professionals between 2020 and 2023. The study included in-depth interviews and we asked questions about these advisors' plans for future organic growth.

Based on the results, the study divided advisors into "growers," "neutrals," and "decliners." Cohort placement was determined by comparing advisors' asset growth to the benchmark for a 60/40 portfolio. The "growers" consistently outpaced market returns in assets under administration for two consecutive years. During the same period, they achieved an average 22% increase in AUA versus market performance of 1.7%.

Our study found that the most successful growth-oriented advisors gravitated toward one of four personas, or character types: "the rainmaker," "the advisor as a CEO," "the financial planner," or "the private wealth specialist." While most growing advisors demonstrate a blend of these personas, our study suggested that growth-oriented advisors gravitate toward one or two of these growth personas in their businesses. Each type embraces distinct behaviors but all prioritize intentional practice design.

Here are the traits and best practices we found in each of these personas:

The rainmaker. These advisors tend to generate new business through relationships and referrals, building connections within centers of influence, such as CPAs, attorneys, and credit unions. Their outreach includes client appreciation and educational events targeting specific industries or demographic groups — events that often lead to connections with future clients. Many belong to formal referral networks, where professionals with complementary skills cross-refer clients and collaborate to meet complex client needs.

Advisor as CEO. These advisors emphasize structure, planning, and long-term scale, managing their practices with the mind-set of a business owner. Unlike peers who may operate more reactively, they follow formal business plans, set growth targets, and track progress against clear metrics such as annual revenue growth or client retention rates. A hallmark of this persona is the adoption of a team-based model, which enables them to deliver a broader range of services and deepen client relationships. On average, these team-led practices oversee $250 million in AUM, compared with just $80 million for solo practitioners. Many also expand capacity by hiring junior advisors or operational staff once they reach certain client thresholds, and they often put succession and continuity plans in place well before they are needed.

The financial planner. This growth persona concentrates on creating more value within their existing client base rather than relying primarily on new client acquisition. They often conduct detailed client discovery meetings, develop written financial plans and consistently update them. Their strategy centers on delivering comprehensive financial advice through structured, repeatable processes that help them strengthen client relationships, capture a greater share of household assets, and drive sustainable growth.

Many of these advisors charge a separate planning fee. Doing so signals to clients that financial planning is a distinct, value-driven service — on par with investment management — rather than a complimentary add-on. When clients pay directly for planning, they tend to engage more deeply in the process and recognize its importance in shaping their long-term success. At the same time, the fee compensates advisors for the considerable time and expertise they invest in creating comprehensive plans. According to the study, this practice can result in 20% to 30% higher asset growth than peers who don't charge a separate fee.

Many who fall into this advisor type also hold advanced credentials, such as the Certified Financial Planner certification from the CFP Board, and regularly participate in peer-led study groups where they share insights and sharpen their skills. Over time, this can help them better serve existing clients and expand wallet share.

The private wealth specialist. Advisors in this category typically serve households with complex planning needs and at least $5 million in investible assets. Their clientele primarily includes business owners, corporate executives, and multigenerational families. They collaborate with estate attorneys, tax professionals and other advisors.

The study found that advisors with the private wealth specialist persona tend to pursue multiple credentials, including the Certified Private Wealth Advisor certification from Investments & Wealth Institute, and apply their technical expertise to support their clients' tax planning, business succession, and executive compensation needs. Examples might include structuring stock option exercises for executives to minimize tax impact, or coordinating business succession strategies that balance liquidity with legacy planning.

These advisors often focus on two or three distinct client segments, including physicians, recently divorced individuals, or retired professionals from specific industries. For instance, one private wealth specialist built strong ties with local automotive manufacturers in Detroit. What started as retirement planning courses for professionals turned into comprehensive wealth planning and implementation as many of these individuals moved up the corporate ladder and into executive suites.

The most successful advisors in our study didn't rely on chance or a single formula. Some may identify closely with one persona. Others will adopt elements from several, consistently adapting to support their clients' changing business needs. By adopting the mind-set and practices that align with their strengths, other advisors can unlock the same potential and shape the future of their business with intention.

Dimple Shah is EVP, strategy & client experience, at Osaic . With over 20 years of experience in financial services, technology, and private equity, she helps advisors optimize operations and develop market solutions. She is a graduate of Harvard Business School and Stanford University.

Editor's note: Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's Advisor newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to advisor.editors@barrons.com .

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
